title: The Bank of New York is founded
who: Edwin G. Burrows and Mike Wallace
work: Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (1998)
take: Burrows and Wallace situate the Bank of New York within the postwar scramble by a merchant-lawyer elite, led by Hamilton, to rebuild the city's commerce and knit New York into the emerging national financial order, laying groundwork for its rise as a capital of money.

title: New York passes gradual abolition
work: In the Shadow of Slavery: African Americans in New York City, 1626–1863 (2003)
take: Harris argues that gradual emancipation freed no one immediately and bound children of enslaved mothers to lengthy indentures, so that by the end of abolition whites had legally and socially recast Black New Yorkers as a separate, dependent and unequal group.

title: Aaron Burr charters the Manhattan Company
take: Koeppel shows the Manhattan Company was essentially a ruse: ostensibly chartered to bring clean water to a fever-ravaged city, its real prize was a banking charter, and its failure to supply adequate water became the chief obstacle delaying a genuine municipal water system for decades.

title: Slavery ends in New York State
work: In the Shadow of Slavery: African Americans in New York City, 1626–1863 (2003)
take: Harris traces how emancipation in 1827 did not end inequality but coincided with hardening racial lines, as free Black New Yorkers built voluntary associations and a moral-reform politics shaped by class distinctions within their own community.

title: The Erie Canal opens
work: The Artificial River: The Erie Canal and the Paradox of Progress, 1817–1862 (1996)
take: Sheriff argues the canal embodied a "paradox of progress": ordinary people eagerly embraced the trade, goods and mobility it delivered even as they grew ambivalent about the environmental damage, market dependence and social costs that came with it.

title: Barnum opens his American Museum
work: E Pluribus Barnum: The Great Showman and the Making of U.S. Popular Culture (1997)
take: Adams argues Barnum's museum and spectacles negotiated the era's conflicts over slavery, immigration, feminism and capitalism, blending high-culture aspiration with commercialism in ways that helped shape an emerging American class structure and mass culture.

title: Croton water reaches Manhattan
take: Koeppel presents the Croton Aqueduct as a triumph of political will as much as engineering, arguing that only the shock of the 1832 cholera epidemic and the 1835 Great Fire finally overcame entrenched private and financial interests blocking a public water supply.

title: Famine Irish transform the city
work: Five Points: The 19th-Century New York City Neighborhood That Invented Tap Dance, Stole Elections, and Became the World's Most Notorious Slum (2001)
take: Drawing on bank records, police reports and archaeology, Anbinder argues that Five Points, home to famine Irish and others, was never as depraved as outsiders claimed but rather embodied the immigrant saga of enduring great hardship on the way to a better life.

title: The Astor Place Riot turns deadly
work: Highbrow/Lowbrow: The Emergence of Cultural Hierarchy in America (1988)
take: Levine reads the Astor Place Riot as a turning point in the "sacralization" of culture, when Shakespeare and opera were being wrested from a shared popular audience into the possession of a self-consciously refined elite, splitting American culture into highbrow and lowbrow.

title: The state sets aside land for Central Park
work: The Park and the People: A History of Central Park (1992)
take: Rosenzweig and Blackmar recovered the human cost of the park's creation, showing that the land was taken by eminent domain from existing communities, including the Black landowners of Seneca Village, whose displacement their book helped restore to public memory.

title: Castle Garden becomes an immigrant depot
work: City of Dreams: The 400-Year Epic History of Immigrant New York (2016)
take: Anbinder recounts how Castle Garden served as America's first official immigrant depot, a state-run gateway that screened and aided millions of newcomers and prefigured the far larger federal station later built at Ellis Island.

title: The Greensward Plan wins Central Park
work: The Park and the People: A History of Central Park (1992)
take: The authors argue Central Park was less a single designer's vision than a contested social project, built by immigrant and Irish laborers and continually fought over by merchants, politicians and reformers who disputed how the park should look and who it was for.

title: The Draft Riots convulse the city
work: The New York City Draft Riots: Their Significance for American Society and Politics in the Age of the Civil War (1990)
take: Bernstein argues the riots were not a mere spasm of Irish mob violence but a revealing political event rooted in class, ethnic and racial fault lines, whose aftermath reshaped labor, elite reform and the rise of machine politics under Tammany.

title: The Tweed Ring is exposed
work: Boss Tweed: The Rise and Fall of the Corrupt Pol Who Conceived the Soul of Modern New York (2005)
take: Ackerman offers a more nuanced Tweed, granting that his ring elevated corruption to "stunning proportions" while also arguing that Tweed championed immigrants and the poor, won home rule for the city and backed public works, so that "except for his stealing, Tweed would have been a great man."

title: The dumbbell tenement law
who: Edwin G. Burrows and Mike Wallace
work: Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (1998)
take: Burrows and Wallace treat the 1879 law and its dumbbell design as a well-meant reform that backfired, its narrow airshafts breeding filth and disease and making the tenement the emblem of Lower East Side overcrowding and of the long, unresolved fight over housing the poor.

title: The Brooklyn Bridge opens
work: The Great Bridge: The Epic Story of the Building of the Brooklyn Bridge (1972)
take: McCullough frames the bridge as a fourteen-year human epic of engineering, corruption and endurance, centered on the Roeblings, and on Emily Warren Roebling's crucial role directing the work after Washington Roebling was disabled by caisson disease.

title: Riis publishes How the Other Half Lives
who: Edwin G. Burrows and Mike Wallace
work: Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (1998)
take: Burrows and Wallace place Riis's flash-lit exposé within the wider Gilded Age reckoning over tenement poverty, treating it as a landmark of muckraking that pushed respectable New York and reformers like Theodore Roosevelt toward housing and settlement reform.

title: The Greater New York charter is signed
work: Power and Society: Greater New York at the Turn of the Century (1982)
take: Hammack argues consolidation was shaped not by a single dominant elite but by shifting competition and alliances among economic, political and social groups; the result was the strong-mayor, five-borough government and the Tammany-plus-bureaucracy order that ran the city for decades.
